ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pandan-flavoured bread with peanut butter; mapped to closest parent class 'bread food product' (FOODON_03315763).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pandan bread with peanut butter; A000N (Bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pandan bread with peanut butter; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is 2 slices of pandan bread (approximately 80 g) spread with peanut butter, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pandan bread with peanut butter — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pandan bread with peanut butter (cr_id F05020077). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pandan bread with peanut butter. GI estimated at 70 based on white bread (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). Pandan flavouring does not materially alter the starch composition. Peanut butter's fat and protein content partially moderates postprandial glucose response; however, the dominant carbohydrate matrix is refined wheat flour, supporting a GI of approximately 70. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 40.44 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 22.6 per 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 337 mg/100 g (borderline high); partially offset by dietary fibre 4.1 g/100 g and potassium 109 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to one serving (80 g) to keep sodium intake below 270 mg per meal; choose unsalted peanut butter and avoid adding salty condiments to reduce cumulative sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~70) refined wheat flour base with 40.4 g carbohydrate/100 g; carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio of approximately 10:1 is unfavourable for glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one slice (40 g), pair with a non-starchy vegetable, and preferably substitute with wholemeal pandan bread to lower the glycaemic index and improve the carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g is within acceptable range; peanut butter contributes MUFA-dominant unsaturated fat and fibre 4.1 g/100 g which are cardioprotective; sodium 337 mg/100 g requires monitoring
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pandan bread with peanut butter can be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions (80 g); use natural unsalted peanut butter free of hydrogenated oils to avoid trans fat and excess sodium, and pair with fruit or vegetables to increase potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Peanut butter provides MUFA-dominant fat, vitamin E, and polyphenols; fibre 4.1 g/100 g exceeds the 3 g/100 g positive criterion; saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g is well below the 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed in moderate portions; choose unsalted natural peanut butter without hydrogenated oils to avoid trans fat and excess sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pandan bread with peanut butter is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack item consisting of sliced soft white bread flavoured with pandan (screwpine leaf) extract, spread with peanut butter. Pandan gives the bread a distinctive green hue and a subtly sweet, floral, vanilla-like aroma characteristic of Southeast Asian baking. The bread is soft and pillowy in texture, while the peanut butter adds a creamy, nutty richness. Per 100 g, this food provides 324 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 11.6 g fat, 40.4 g carbohydrate, 4.1 g dietary fibre, 3.7 g sugar, and 337 mg sodium. The relatively high protein content is contributed by both the bread and the peanut butter. Dietary fibre at 4.1 g/100 g is notable for a bread-based product. Clinically, the refined wheat flour base carries a high glycaemic index (~70), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 337 mg/100 g is borderline and warrants attention for those with hypertension. The peanut butter component contributes monounsaturated fats, vitamin E, and polyphenols that are beneficial for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. This dish is widely available at kopitiams (traditional Singaporean coffee shops), hawker centres, and bakeries across Singapore, and is commonly paired with kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020077) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-defined Singaporean product with an exact match in the primary regional approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pandan bread with peanut butter is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite product. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the reported fields not available in the HPB Foodo database for this composite food item as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in standard pandan bread with natural peanut butter formulations per HPB source context. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB (plant-based ingredients; no animal fat). saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from the peanut butter component contribution plus bread fat fraction; consistent with total fat of 11.57 g/100 g and the MUFA-dominant fat profile of peanut butter. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for pandan bread with peanut butter was found in approved sources. Glycaemic load per 80 g serving = (70 × 40.44 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 22.6. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as no exact ontology term exists for this specific composite food; gap notes provided for both.
